questions.question: Why is using AsyncStorage to store JWT access tokens a security vulnerability, and what is the correct approach?
options: AsyncStorage is too slow for JWT storage — MMKV should be used instead.

AsyncStorage stores data as unencrypted plain text in a file accessible to anyone with physical device access or root/jailbreak access. A JWT access token in AsyncStorage can be extracted from a backed-up device (iTunes backup, adb backup), rooted Android device, or jailbroken iOS device. Expo SecureStore uses iOS Keychain and Android Keystore — hardware-backed encrypted storage that is accessible ONLY to your app and cannot be extracted even on rooted/jailbroken devices.
AsyncStorage tokens expire after 24 hours automatically — you must use SecureStore for persistence.
The vulnerability is theoretical — no real attacks exploit AsyncStorage on mobile.
questions.answer: AsyncStorage stores data as unencrypted plain text in a file accessible to anyone with physical device access or root/jailbreak access. A JWT access token in AsyncStorage can be extracted from a backed-up device (iTunes backup, adb backup), rooted Android device, or jailbroken iOS device. Expo SecureStore uses iOS Keychain and Android Keystore — hardware-backed encrypted storage that is accessible ONLY to your app and cannot be extracted even on rooted/jailbroken devices.
questions.explanation: This is a real attack vector: 'Juice jacking' (malicious USB charger) + phone backup extraction can steal AsyncStorage data. PCI DSS compliance requires payment tokens in hardware-backed storage. For most apps: use SecureStore for JWT tokens and any sensitive credentials, AsyncStorage only for non-sensitive preferences.

questions.question: In the Axios token refresh interceptor, why must pendingRequests queue other 401 requests while token refresh is in progress?
options: Queuing is a performance optimization to prevent duplicate API calls within 1 second.

Without the queue: multiple parallel API calls fail simultaneously with 401 → each independently triggers a token refresh → multiple refresh requests hit the backend → refresh tokens may be single-use (rotated) → second refresh attempt fails (token already used) → user is logged out despite the first refresh succeeding. The queue prevents this 'refresh storm': the first 401 triggers ONE refresh, all others wait for it to complete, then replay with the new token.
The queue prevents the refresh endpoint from being called more than once per second.
Queuing 401 requests is required by OAuth 2.0 specification RFC 6749.
questions.answer: Without the queue: multiple parallel API calls fail simultaneously with 401 → each independently triggers a token refresh → multiple refresh requests hit the backend → refresh tokens may be single-use (rotated) → second refresh attempt fails (token already used) → user is logged out despite the first refresh succeeding. The queue prevents this 'refresh storm': the first 401 triggers ONE refresh, all others wait for it to complete, then replay with the new token.
questions.explanation: This is a race condition that becomes more likely as apps make more parallel API calls (React Query prefetching, parallel data fetches). The isRefreshing flag + pendingRequests queue is the standard pattern for single-instance refresh with multiple-waiter replay. Production apps must implement this to avoid phantom logouts.

questions.question: Apple's App Store requires Sign in with Apple if your app offers ANY third-party social login (Google, Facebook, etc.). What is the technical reason developers must implement this?
options: Apple Sign-In is optional — it's only recommended, not required.

Apple's App Store Review Guideline 4.8 explicitly states: apps that offer third-party social login must also offer Sign in with Apple as an option. Technically: iOS developers must import expo-apple-authentication, implement the sign-in flow, send the identityToken to their backend for verification using Apple's public keys (JWT verification), and handle the first-time-only name/email fields. Apps that skip this are rejected during review. The guideline exists to give users a privacy-preserving login option without sharing email with third parties.
Apple Sign-In is only required for apps in the Health & Fitness category.
Sign in with Apple is mandatory only for new apps — existing apps with Google login are exempt.
questions.answer: Apple's App Store Review Guideline 4.8 explicitly states: apps that offer third-party social login must also offer Sign in with Apple as an option. Technically: iOS developers must import expo-apple-authentication, implement the sign-in flow, send the identityToken to their backend for verification using Apple's public keys (JWT verification), and handle the first-time-only name/email fields. Apps that skip this are rejected during review. The guideline exists to give users a privacy-preserving login option without sharing email with third parties.
questions.explanation: Apple Sign-In also offers 'Hide My Email' — Apple generates a proxy email address (e.g., randomstring@privaterelay.appleid.com) and forwards to the real email. Your backend receives the proxy. This is the privacy feature Apple designed this requirement around. Backend note: use Apple's REST API or a library like apple-auth to verify identityToken.
